story.title: Dr. Chen and the Dissolving Puzzle
story.text:
Hello! I’m Dr. Sarah Chen, and I’m a Research Scientist at the National Science Lab. My job is to understand complex systems, which are like puzzles with many moving parts. One of the puzzles I study is dissolving—the process of a solid breaking apart and mixing evenly into a liquid.

Have you ever noticed how sugar disappears faster in hot tea than in cold lemonade? That’s the puzzle in action! Everything is made of tiny particles, too small for us to see. When you put a sugar cube in water, the water particles start bumping into it. These bumps break the sugar apart, piece by piece, until it spreads out evenly. When that happens, you’ve made a solution!

So, why does temperature matter? Warmer water means faster-moving water particles. They have more energy, so they bump into the sugar more often and with more force. This increases the Particle Contact Rate, which is just a sciencey way of saying how often the particles collide. More contact means a faster dissolving speed!

Stirring your drink also speeds things up. It pushes fresh water particles towards the sugar, increasing the contact rate. The faster the sugar dissolves, the more quickly the concentration—how much sugar is in the water—increases. Understanding these connections helps my team develop everything from fast-acting medicines to instant drink mixes. We’re detectives for the invisible world of particles!
think_abouts: Think about making a drink. What could you do to make a sugar cube dissolve faster?
What do you think would happen to the dissolving speed if you didn't stir the water at all?

margin_defs.definition: When a solid breaks apart in a liquid.
The tiny, invisible pieces of things.
An even mixture of a solid in a liquid.
How much solid is dissolved in the liquid.

check_prompts: According to Dr. Chen, what are two things you can do to increase dissolving speed?

vocabulary.definition: The process of a solid breaking apart and mixing evenly into a liquid
vocabulary.use_sentence: The dissolving of the salt in the warm water happened very quickly.

vocabulary.definition: A mixture where one substance is completely dissolved in another
vocabulary.use_sentence: Making lemonade creates a solution of sugar, lemon juice, and water.

vocabulary.definition: How much dissolved substance is in a given amount of liquid
vocabulary.use_sentence: Adding another spoonful of sugar will increase the concentration of the sweet tea.

vocabulary.definition: The tiny pieces that make up all matter, too small to see
vocabulary.use_sentence: All solids, liquids, and gases are made of tiny, moving particles.

discussion_cards.prompt: Dr. Chen described dissolving as a system. Besides making drinks, where else in your daily life might you see this dissolving system at work?
discussion_cards.frame: One place I see dissolving is when...

discussion_cards.prompt: If you wanted to design an experiment to test how stirring affects dissolving speed, what steps would you take?
discussion_cards.frame: To test how stirring affects dissolving, first I would...

reflection_frames.knew: Before reading, I already knew that ___
reflection_frames.learned: From the story, I learned that ___
